title: Woman Leader: Chaupal to Impact
description: Discussion capture, Micro Improvement planning, and impact capture linked across time.

steps.text: I just finished a Shiksha Chaupal in my village. I want to note down what we discussed.
Sure, Savitri. I'll help you capture the Chaupal discussion. What was the main concern that came up?
Parents said girls feel unsafe travelling to the secondary school, so many are dropping out after Class 6.
That's an important concern. Who raised it, and has anyone tried to address it before?
Mostly mothers in the group. A few of us spoke to the school last year, but nothing changed.
Understood. I've structured this as a documented Chaupal record.

blocks.title: Chaupal Discussion Record
blocks.body:
Location: Savitri's village
Issue: Girls feel unsafe travelling to the secondary school; several have dropped out after Class 6
Raised by: Mothers in the community group
Prior action: Raised with the school last year, no change made
Status: Documented, no active plan yet

steps.text: Is there anything else from the discussion you'd like to add?
No, that covers it.
Great, this discussion is saved. Is there anything else I can help with today?
Parents don't feel their daughters are safe travelling to secondary school - can you help me make a plan for this?
steps.contextNotice: Context switched to Saathi. Carrying forward the Chaupal discussion on girls' school safety.

steps.text: Thanks, Savitri - I already have the context from your Chaupal discussion: girls in your village feel unsafe travelling to the secondary school after Class 6, and an earlier request to the school didn't lead to change. Let's build a Micro Improvement plan for this. What's one small action the community could try in the next two weeks?
We could organise a group walk with a few mothers escorting the girls on the days classes run late.
That's a strong first step. Here is your Micro Improvement plan.

rows.label: Small action
rows.value: Mothers form a rotating escort group for the walk to and from school on late-class days

rows.label: Linked to
rows.value: Chaupal discussion on girls' school safety

rows.label: Expected outcome
rows.value: Girls who had stopped attending return for at least 3 days a week

rows.label: Timeline
rows.value: 6 weeks

rows.label: Success signal
rows.value: Attendance of at-risk girls tracked weekly by the group

rows.label: Support needed
rows.value: 3-4 mothers willing to rotate escort duty

steps.text: I'll check back with you in a few weeks to see how this went. Is there anything else you need right now?
It's been six weeks. The mothers' walking group worked - I want to record what changed.
steps.contextNotice: Context switched to Mitra. Linking this update to your Chaupal discussion and Micro Improvement plan.

steps.text: Wonderful to hear, Savitri. Since this links to your Chaupal discussion and Micro Improvement plan, I only need the impact details. What changed?
Three girls who had stopped attending are back in school, and two more mothers have joined the walking group.
This is now a complete, linked journey.

blocks.title: Linked Journey Summary
blocks.body:
Discussion: Girls feel unsafe travelling to the secondary school after Class 6; raised at the Chaupal by mothers in the community.

Plan: A rotating mothers' escort group for late-class days, with attendance of at-risk girls tracked weekly.

Impact: Three girls who had stopped attending are back in school, and two more mothers have joined the escort group.

steps.text: This is now saved as a complete, linked journey - from discussion to plan to impact. Is there anything else I can help with today?

title: School Leader: Story to Next MI
description: A completed improvement story becomes the starting point for the next Micro Improvement.

demoScripts.title: School Leader: Story to Next MI

steps.text: I want to tell you about how we improved attendance by involving parents.
I'd love to hear it, Rajesh. What was the challenge you were facing at your school?
Attendance had dropped, especially after the harvest season. Many parents didn't see why regular attendance mattered.
What action did you take?
We started a monthly parent engagement drive - home visits, and a simple attendance chart shared with parents each week.
What result did you see, and what did you learn?
Attendance improved by around 18% over two months. I learned that parents respond better to seeing the data than to general reminders.
That's a great story. I've structured it as your improvement story card.

rows.label: Challenge
rows.value: Attendance dropped after harvest season; parents didn't see why regular attendance mattered

rows.label: Action taken
rows.value: Monthly parent engagement drive with home visits and a weekly attendance chart

rows.label: Outcome
rows.value: Attendance improved by around 18% over two months

rows.label: Reflection
rows.value: Parents respond better to seeing data than to general reminders

steps.text: Saved. Is there anything else you'd like to add?
No, that's it for now.

Based on what you've shared, your school still has open challenges around learning outcomes and teacher motivation that came up in your story. Would you like to explore what challenge you can solve next, or get help on one you've already identified?
Yes, let's look at classroom learning practices next.
steps.contextNotice: Context switched to Saathi. Carrying forward your improvement story.

steps.text: Good choice, Rajesh. I already know your school and what you tried with attendance. For classroom learning, what specific gap are you noticing right now?
Students can recite answers but struggle to explain concepts in their own words.
That's a clear starting point. Here's a Micro Improvement plan for classroom learning practices, grounded in your attendance story.

rows.label: Small action
rows.value: Run a weekly 'explain it back' routine where students restate concepts in their own words

rows.label: Built on
rows.value: Attendance improvement story (data-driven parent engagement)

rows.label: Expected outcome
rows.value: More students can explain concepts without prompting

rows.label: Timeline
rows.value: 3 weeks

rows.label: Success signal
rows.value: Teacher log shows increase in students explaining unprompted

rows.label: Support needed
rows.value: One classroom observation per week from a peer teacher

steps.text: You now have a concrete next MI, grown directly out of your own story. Anything else you'd like help with?

title: Teacher: Video Insight to Lesson Plan
description: A Video Analyzer Agent's teaching-model analysis becomes the Teacher Companion's lesson plan input.

demoScripts.title: Teacher: Video Insight to Lesson Plan

steps.text: I have a video of a really good classroom session on fractions. I want to create a lesson plan for linear equations in the same style.
Got it - this needs two steps: understanding what made that video effective, then building your new lesson plan around it. Let me start with the video.
Here you go - sharing the fractions video now.
steps.contextNotice: Context switched to Video Analyzer Agent.

steps.text: Thanks, Meena. Analysing the teaching session now...
Here's what I found in the fractions video.

blocks.title: Teaching Model Analysis
items: Pedagogical approach: concepts broken into a visual fraction-bar model before introducing symbols
Sequencing: concrete example, then guided practice, then independent practice
Activities: paired fraction-bar cutting, quick-check questions every 5 minutes
Pacing: short 8-10 minute segments with a check-in between each
Engagement moments: students explain their reasoning aloud before the teacher confirms

steps.text: This is great - now can you build my linear equations lesson plan in this style?
steps.contextNotice: Context switched to Teacher Companion. Carrying forward the video analysis.

steps.text: On it. I'll use the same approach - concrete before abstract, short paced segments, paired practice. Just need two details: which grade, and how long is the class?
Grade 8, 40-minute period.
Here's your lesson plan for linear equations, built in the style of the video you shared.

rows.label: Opening
rows.value: Concrete balance-scale model to introduce equality (8 min)

rows.label: Guided practice
rows.value: Paired solving with balance-scale diagrams, quick-check every 5 min (15 min)

rows.label: Independent practice
rows.value: Short problem set solved individually, symbols only (12 min)

rows.label: Pacing
rows.value: Four 8-10 minute segments with a check-in between each

rows.label: Engagement checkpoint
rows.value: Students explain their solving steps aloud before the teacher confirms

steps.text: This should feel familiar to your students, in the same visual, paced style as the fractions session. Anything you'd like adjusted?

title: Program Designer: Insight to Design
description: Insights from a past program become the evidence base for designing a new one.

steps.text: Can you help me understand what worked and what didn't in our teacher mentoring program in Bihar last year?
Looking at the Bihar teacher mentoring program data now...
Here's what the data shows.

blocks.title: What worked
items: Peer-mentor pairing reached a 70% session-completion rate
Monthly cluster meetings improved mentor consistency in accessible blocks

blocks.title: Where the gaps were
items: Mentors in remote blocks visited schools less than 40% as often as those in accessible blocks
No structured way existed to track mentor visit consistency across the program

blocks.title: What the data reveals about teacher needs
items: Teachers asked for more subject-specific coaching, not just general mentoring
Teachers preferred shorter, more frequent visits over long, infrequent ones

steps.text: Based on this, can you help me design a new program for teacher capacity building in Telangana?
steps.contextNotice: Context switched to Program Design Companion. Carrying forward the Bihar program insights.

steps.text: I have the Bihar analysis as our evidence base - strong peer-mentoring, but weak reach in remote areas and a need for subject-specific, more frequent coaching. Let's ground the Telangana design in that. Who is the primary target group for this program?
Secondary school teachers in Telangana, especially in rural mandals.
Good. What's the main objective you want this program to achieve in year one?
Improve subject-specific teaching practices through more frequent, localized mentoring.
Here's your program design, built on the Bihar evidence base.

blocks.title: Program Snapshot: Teacher Capacity Building, Telangana

rows.label: Objective
rows.value: Improve subject-specific teaching practices through frequent, localized mentoring

rows.label: Target group
rows.value: Secondary school teachers in rural Telangana mandals

rows.label: Key activities
rows.value: Mandal-level mentor clusters with subject-specialist coaches; bi-weekly short visits instead of long infrequent ones

rows.label: Indicators
rows.value: Mentor visit consistency by block, teacher-reported coaching relevance, subject-specific practice adoption rate

steps.text: This design directly addresses the two gaps we saw in Bihar - remote reach and subject-specific coaching. Anything you'd like to refine?
